title: Alcoholic Hepatitis
oneLiner: Acute inflammation of liver due to heavy alcohol use; high short-term mortality if severe; need to risk-stratify and rule out sepsis/bleeding.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Sepsis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis / cholangitis)**: fever + abdominal tenderness + rising lactate — do blood cultures and ascitic tap *before* any steroids.
- **Variceal haemorrhage**: haematemesis/melaena + haemodynamic instability — start terlipressin + antibiotics, arrange urgent OGD.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy (Grade III–IV)**: disoriented/aggressive → obtunded — check GCS, protect airway, give lactulose, consider CT head if first episode.
- **Alcohol withdrawal (DTs)**: tremor, autonomic activation, hallucinations — use CIWA-Ar protocol, give benzodiazepines (e.g. chlordiazepoxide) and high-dose IV thiamine (Pabrinex).
- **Hepatorenal syndrome**: oliguria + rising creatinine despite fluid resuscitation — stop diuretics, consider terlipressin + albumin.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** jaundice (rapid onset, deep), malaise, anorexia, right upper quadrant pain/tenderness, fever (if severe)
- **Key exam findings:** tender hepatomegaly (may be small in cirrhotic), splenomegaly, ascites, spider naevi, palmar erythema, Dupuytren's contracture, parotid enlargement, gynaecomastia, foetor hepaticus (late)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Decompensated cirrhosis (non-alcoholic cause)** — more signs of chronic liver disease (caput medusae, muscle wasting) without heavy acute alcohol history
- **Acute cholangitis / cholecystitis** — fever + RUQ pain + positive Murphy’s sign; bilirubin often higher in alkaline phosphatase dominant pattern; ultrasound shows dilated ducts / gallstones
- **Drug-induced liver injury** — recent medication (paracetamol, amoxicillin/clavulanate, statins, herbal remedies); eosinophilia may be present
- **Viral hepatitis (A, B, C, E)** — travel/exposure history; serology positive
- **Acute fatty liver of pregnancy** — third trimester; HELP criteria; urgent liver screen and obstetric referral
- **Splitter:** AST : ALT > 1.5 (alcoholic hepatitis) vs < 1 (viral/drug); also GGT elevated out of proportion to other LFTs in alcohol
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** urine dip (bilirubin + urobilinogen), capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia common), NEWS2, ascitic tap if fluid present (send for culture, neutrophils, albumin)
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, INR, glucose, lactate, blood cultures (if febrile), ammonia (if encephalopathy but not essential), magnesium, phosphate
- **Imaging:** **First-line** – ultrasound abdomen (assess for cirrhosis, ascites, exclude biliary obstruction); **Second-line** – CT abdomen if suspicion of abscess/pancreatitis
- **Special tests:** viral serology (Hep A IgM, B sAg, C Ab, E IgM), autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-LKM, IgG), ceruloplasmin (if <40 years), ferritin/iron studies (if very high), alpha-1-antitrypsin level
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - *Maddrey’s discriminant function (DF)* = 4.6 × (patient PT – control PT) + serum bilirubin (mg/dL). **DF ≥ 32 = severe alcoholic hepatitis** (consider steroids). *Note: convert bilirubin from µmol/L to mg/dL by dividing by 17.1.*
  - *Glasgow Alcoholic Hepatitis Score (GAHS)*: age, WCC, urea, bilirubin, INR (score 0–9; ≥9 predicts poor outcome)
  - *MELD-Na*: used for 90-day mortality (≥21 = severe)
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures and ascitic tap before starting steroids — active infection is a contraindication. Also check pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess severity using DF (calculate within 24h)
  - Give IV Pabrinex (pair of ampoules in 100ml sodium chloride 0.9% or glucose 5% over 30 min) — one pair tds for 3 days, then oral thiamine – to prevent Wernicke’s encephalopathy
  - Manage alcohol withdrawal: CIWA-Ar protocol, chlordiazepoxide (see local policy)
  - Correct hypoglycaemia (IV glucose 10% if needed)
  - If febrile, start empirical antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam or cefotaxime) after cultures
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If DF ≥ 32 and no active infection / GI bleed / renal failure → start **prednisolone 40mg PO once daily for 28 days** (or [check local policy] for dose). Then reassess at day 7; if Lille score >0.45, stop steroids as non-responsive.
  - Nutritional support: high-protein, high-calorie diet (30–35 kcal/kg/day); nasogastric feeding if unable to eat; daily multivitamins (including thiamine)
  - Restrict sodium to <2g/day if ascites; spironolactone 100mg once daily (titrate) – but avoid in hepatorenal syndrome
  - Monitor daily: weight, abdominal girth, urine output, LFT/INR, U&E, glucose
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abstinence counselling (alcohol liaison team referral)
  - Hepatology follow-up at 2–4 weeks post discharge; repeat DF/MELD to assess progression
  - Vaccination: Hep B, Hep A, pneumococcal, influenza (if cirrhotic)
  - Screening for oesophageal varices if cirrhosis confirmed (OGD)
  - Bone density if long-term steroid use or chronic liver disease
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 5% dextrose + Pabrinex (avoid large volumes of normal saline if ascitic); maintain euvolemia (no overloading)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Use LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily) only if platelets >50, no active bleeding; consider intermittent compression stockings if high bleeding risk
  - **Diet:** Normal diet with high protein + vitamin B-complex supplements; NBM only if OGD planned or encephalopathy
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (with GCS and capillary glucose); daily weight, abdominal girth, urine output
sections.Drugs & Doses:
| Drug | Dose | Route | Duration | Notes |
|------|------|-------|----------|-------|
| **Pabrinex** | Pair of ampoules (1× thiamine HCl 250mg + 1× ascorbic acid 500mg + glucose 1g) | IV in 100ml NaCl/glucose over 30 min | 3 times daily for 3 days then oral thiamine | Always give before glucose to prevent Wernicke's |
| **Prednisolone** | 40mg | PO | 28 days (then stop, no taper) | Only if DF ≥32 no contraindications; check for active infection/bleed/renal failure |
| **Chlordiazepoxide** | [check local CIWA-Ar protocol] e.g. 10–50mg q6h PRN | PO (or IV if unable) | Taper over 5–7 days | Avoid in severe encephalopathy (can worsen confusion) |
| **Spironolactone** | 100mg | PO | Once daily, titrate to max 400mg | Contraindicated in hepatorenal syndrome; check K+ |
| **Terlipressin** | [check local policy] 0.5–2mg IV q4–6h | IV | For acute variceal bleed or hepatorenal syndrome | Requires monitoring of cardiac/ischaemic effects |
| **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Reduce all hepatically metabolised drugs (e.g. opioids, sedatives) by 50% if severe impairment; avoid NSAIDs and metformin. | | | | |
| **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving prednisolone without screening for active infection** (can precipitate fatal sepsis). Always wait for blood cultures and ascitic tap results. | | | | |
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Variceal bleeding, hepatorenal syndrome, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, encephalopathy, sepsis, alcohol withdrawal, hypoglycaemia, pancreatitis
- **Chronic:** Cirrhosis, portal hypertension, hepatocellular carcinoma, malnutrition, osteoporosis, peripheral neuropathy
sections.Prognosis:
- **Severe (DF ≥32, MELD ≥21):** 30–50% mortality at 1 month. Non-responders to steroids (Lille >0.45) have >80% mortality at 6 months without transplant.
- **Mild/moderate:** 80% improve with abstinence; but 5-year progression to cirrhosis in 30–50% if drinking continues.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Calculate Maddrey’s DF on admission** – guides steroid decision and identifies severe cases early.
- **Do ascitic tap** in any patient with ascites – rule out SBP before starting steroids.
- **Start Pabrinex** before any glucose-containing fluid – prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t give steroids if DF <32** – no benefit, increased infection risk.
- **Don’t give steroids if active infection / GI bleed / renal failure** – treat these first.
- **Don’t forget to manage alcohol withdrawal** – untreated DTs can kill.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening jaundice (darker urine, paler stool), any vomiting of blood or black stools, confusion/aggression, abdominal swelling/pain, fever.
- **Follow-up:** Hepatology outpatient within 2 weeks; repeat LFT/INR; arrange alcohol liaison support.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, GCS ≤13/15, new confusion/coma (encephalopathy), haematemesis or melaena, oliguria (despite fluids) + rising creatinine, lactate >4 mmol/L.
- **Specific thresholds:** INR >3 (uncorrectable), bilirubin >500 µmol/L, creatinine >200 µmol/L, platelet count <20×10⁹/L with active bleeding.

rawMarkdown:
## Alcoholic Hepatitis
**One-liner:** Acute inflammation of liver due to heavy alcohol use; high short-term mortality if severe; need to risk-stratify and rule out sepsis/bleeding.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Sepsis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis / cholangitis)**: fever + abdominal tenderness + rising lactate — do blood cultures and ascitic tap *before* any steroids.
- **Variceal haemorrhage**: haematemesis/melaena + haemodynamic instability — start terlipressin + antibiotics, arrange urgent OGD.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy (Grade III–IV)**: disoriented/aggressive → obtunded — check GCS, protect airway, give lactulose, consider CT head if first episode.
- **Alcohol withdrawal (DTs)**: tremor, autonomic activation, hallucinations — use CIWA-Ar protocol, give benzodiazepines (e.g. chlordiazepoxide) and high-dose IV thiamine (Pabrinex).
- **Hepatorenal syndrome**: oliguria + rising creatinine despite fluid resuscitation — stop diuretics, consider terlipressin + albumin.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** jaundice (rapid onset, deep), malaise, anorexia, right upper quadrant pain/tenderness, fever (if severe)
- **Key exam findings:** tender hepatomegaly (may be small in cirrhotic), splenomegaly, ascites, spider naevi, palmar erythema, Dupuytren's contracture, parotid enlargement, gynaecomastia, foetor hepaticus (late)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Decompensated cirrhosis (non-alcoholic cause)** — more signs of chronic liver disease (caput medusae, muscle wasting) without heavy acute alcohol history
- **Acute cholangitis / cholecystitis** — fever + RUQ pain + positive Murphy’s sign; bilirubin often higher in alkaline phosphatase dominant pattern; ultrasound shows dilated ducts / gallstones
- **Drug-induced liver injury** — recent medication (paracetamol, amoxicillin/clavulanate, statins, herbal remedies); eosinophilia may be present
- **Viral hepatitis (A, B, C, E)** — travel/exposure history; serology positive
- **Acute fatty liver of pregnancy** — third trimester; HELP criteria; urgent liver screen and obstetric referral
- **Splitter:** AST : ALT > 1.5 (alcoholic hepatitis) vs < 1 (viral/drug); also GGT elevated out of proportion to other LFTs in alcohol

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** urine dip (bilirubin + urobilinogen), capillary glucose (hypoglycaemia common), NEWS2, ascitic tap if fluid present (send for culture, neutrophils, albumin)
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, INR, glucose, lactate, blood cultures (if febrile), ammonia (if encephalopathy but not essential), magnesium, phosphate
- **Imaging:** **First-line** – ultrasound abdomen (assess for cirrhosis, ascites, exclude biliary obstruction); **Second-line** – CT abdomen if suspicion of abscess/pancreatitis
- **Special tests:** viral serology (Hep A IgM, B sAg, C Ab, E IgM), autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-LKM, IgG), ceruloplasmin (if <40 years), ferritin/iron studies (if very high), alpha-1-antitrypsin level
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - *Maddrey’s discriminant function (DF)* = 4.6 × (patient PT – control PT) + serum bilirubin (mg/dL). **DF ≥ 32 = severe alcoholic hepatitis** (consider steroids). *Note: convert bilirubin from µmol/L to mg/dL by dividing by 17.1.*
  - *Glasgow Alcoholic Hepatitis Score (GAHS)*: age, WCC, urea, bilirubin, INR (score 0–9; ≥9 predicts poor outcome)
  - *MELD-Na*: used for 90-day mortality (≥21 = severe)
- **Never-miss test:** Blood cultures and ascitic tap before starting steroids — active infection is a contraindication. Also check pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess severity using DF (calculate within 24h)
  - Give IV Pabrinex (pair of ampoules in 100ml sodium chloride 0.9% or glucose 5% over 30 min) — one pair tds for 3 days, then oral thiamine – to prevent Wernicke’s encephalopathy
  - Manage alcohol withdrawal: CIWA-Ar protocol, chlordiazepoxide (see local policy)
  - Correct hypoglycaemia (IV glucose 10% if needed)
  - If febrile, start empirical antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam or cefotaxime) after cultures
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If DF ≥ 32 and no active infection / GI bleed / renal failure → start **prednisolone 40mg PO once daily for 28 days** (or [check local policy] for dose). Then reassess at day 7; if Lille score >0.45, stop steroids as non-responsive.
  - Nutritional support: high-protein, high-calorie diet (30–35 kcal/kg/day); nasogastric feeding if unable to eat; daily multivitamins (including thiamine)
  - Restrict sodium to <2g/day if ascites; spironolactone 100mg once daily (titrate) – but avoid in hepatorenal syndrome
  - Monitor daily: weight, abdominal girth, urine output, LFT/INR, U&E, glucose
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Abstinence counselling (alcohol liaison team referral)
  - Hepatology follow-up at 2–4 weeks post discharge; repeat DF/MELD to assess progression
  - Vaccination: Hep B, Hep A, pneumococcal, influenza (if cirrhotic)
  - Screening for oesophageal varices if cirrhosis confirmed (OGD)
  - Bone density if long-term steroid use or chronic liver disease
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 5% dextrose + Pabrinex (avoid large volumes of normal saline if ascitic); maintain euvolemia (no overloading)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Use LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily) only if platelets >50, no active bleeding; consider intermittent compression stockings if high bleeding risk
  - **Diet:** Normal diet with high protein + vitamin B-complex supplements; NBM only if OGD planned or encephalopathy
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (with GCS and capillary glucose); daily weight, abdominal girth, urine output

### Drugs & Doses
| Drug | Dose | Route | Duration | Notes |
|------|------|-------|----------|-------|
| **Pabrinex** | Pair of ampoules (1× thiamine HCl 250mg + 1× ascorbic acid 500mg + glucose 1g) | IV in 100ml NaCl/glucose over 30 min | 3 times daily for 3 days then oral thiamine | Always give before glucose to prevent Wernicke's |
| **Prednisolone** | 40mg | PO | 28 days (then stop, no taper) | Only if DF ≥32 no contraindications; check for active infection/bleed/renal failure |
| **Chlordiazepoxide** | [check local CIWA-Ar protocol] e.g. 10–50mg q6h PRN | PO (or IV if unable) | Taper over 5–7 days | Avoid in severe encephalopathy (can worsen confusion) |
| **Spironolactone** | 100mg | PO | Once daily, titrate to max 400mg | Contraindicated in hepatorenal syndrome; check K+ |
| **Terlipressin** | [check local policy] 0.5–2mg IV q4–6h | IV | For acute variceal bleed or hepatorenal syndrome | Requires monitoring of cardiac/ischaemic effects |
| **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Reduce all hepatically metabolised drugs (e.g. opioids, sedatives) by 50% if severe impairment; avoid NSAIDs and metformin. | | | | |
| **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving prednisolone without screening for active infection** (can precipitate fatal sepsis). Always wait for blood cultures and ascitic tap results. | | | | |

### Complications
- **Acute:** Variceal bleeding, hepatorenal syndrome, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, encephalopathy, sepsis, alcohol withdrawal, hypoglycaemia, pancreatitis
- **Chronic:** Cirrhosis, portal hypertension, hepatocellular carcinoma, malnutrition, osteoporosis, peripheral neuropathy

### Prognosis
- **Severe (DF ≥32, MELD ≥21):** 30–50% mortality at 1 month. Non-responders to steroids (Lille >0.45) have >80% mortality at 6 months without transplant.
- **Mild/moderate:** 80% improve with abstinence; but 5-year progression to cirrhosis in 30–50% if drinking continues.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Calculate Maddrey’s DF on admission** – guides steroid decision and identifies severe cases early.
- **Do ascitic tap** in any patient with ascites – rule out SBP before starting steroids.
- **Start Pabrinex** before any glucose-containing fluid – prevents Wernicke's encephalopathy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t give steroids if DF <32** – no benefit, increased infection risk.
- **Don’t give steroids if active infection / GI bleed / renal failure** – treat these first.
- **Don’t forget to manage alcohol withdrawal** – untreated DTs can kill.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening jaundice (darker urine, paler stool), any vomiting of blood or black stools, confusion/aggression, abdominal swelling/pain, fever.
- **Follow-up:** Hepatology outpatient within 2 weeks; repeat LFT/INR; arrange alcohol liaison support.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:** NEWS2 ≥5, GCS ≤13/15, new confusion/coma (encephalopathy), haematemesis or melaena, oliguria (despite fluids) + rising creatinine, lactate >4 mmol/L.
- **Specific thresholds:** INR >3 (uncorrectable), bilirubin >500 µmol/L, creatinine >200 µmol/L, platelet count <20×10⁹/L with active bleeding.
